---
title: "Audio Batch Workflows"
subtitle: "Turn a collection of short WAV clips into comparable feature matrices and optional file summaries."
sidebar: concepts
---

## The job this workflow is designed for

Use the audio batch workflow when each WAV file is one observation—for
example, one spoken response, sound effect, or short recording—and you want
the same acoustic columns for every file.

`extract_audio_files()` accepts an explicit list of paths.
`extract_audio_dir()` first finds paths in a directory with a pattern such as
`*.wav`, then runs the same workflow. Both return an `AudioBatchResult` with
one `AudioFileResult` per file.

## What happens to each file

The workflow reads the WAV file, computes the selected low-level feature
families, and samples those families onto the time step given by
`resolution_s`. With `resolution_s=0.25`, the output has one time row every
quarter second. The default resampling method is `mean`, so values inside each
time bin are averaged.

This resampling is deliberate and local to the batch workflow. It differs from
the native-grid handoff described in [Native time and clocks](native-time-and-clocks.qmd),
where no common grid is chosen.

Columns are prefixed with their feature family. An RMS column might be named
`rms.rms`; an MFCC column begins with `mfcc.`. The prefix prevents similarly
named measurements from different extractors from colliding.

## Available feature families

| Name | What it contributes |
|---|---|
| `rms` | Root-mean-square signal energy |
| `mel` | Mel-scaled spectral bands |
| `mfcc` | Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients and, by default, their deltas |
| `spectral_stats` | Summary measurements of the spectrum |
| `vad` | Energy-based voice activity values |
| `opensmile_egemaps` | eGeMAPS low-level descriptors through the optional openSMILE backend |

If `selected_features` is omitted, the workflow uses `rms`, `mfcc`,
`spectral_stats`, and `vad`.

## The three output views

Each file result always contains `times_s`, `feature_names`, and a numeric
`matrix`. The first matrix axis follows time; the second follows feature name.

With `as_dataframe=True`, `long_dataframe` combines all file matrices and adds
`file_id` and `time_s` columns. This view preserves the time course.

With `collapse` set, the workflow also summarizes each feature column across
all times in a file. The supported summaries are `mean`, `sd`, `min`, and
`max`, and several may be requested together, as in `mean+sd`. The resulting
`collapsed_dataframe` has one row per file.

Collapsing discards within-file timing. It is appropriate only when the next
analysis needs one fixed-length vector per recording.

## A representative call

```python
from natural_features import extract_audio_dir

result = extract_audio_dir(
    "clips/",
    pattern="*.wav",
    resolution_s=0.25,
    selected_features=["rms", "mfcc", "vad"],
    collapse="mean+sd",
)
```

The [audio batch cookbook](../cookbook/audio-batch-from-wav.qmd) contains a
runnable example that creates a WAV file and inspects both output tables.

## Errors and boundaries

`resolution_s` must be positive. Unknown feature names and unsupported
collapse statistics are rejected before a misleading output can be assembled.
Table output requires pandas. `AudioStimulus.from_wav()` accepts WAV input; the
batch workflow is not a general decoder for MP3, video containers, or remote
media.

Optional feature families still follow the requested [execution
mode](execution-modes.qmd). The batch wrapper coordinates files and columns; it
does not change the scientific meaning of a feature-computing method or install
the package, model, or program that implements it.

## See also

[Extract audio features from WAV files](../cookbook/audio-batch-from-wav.qmd) · [Execution modes](execution-modes.qmd)
